title: Heatwave continues in Bihar with temperatures above 40°C, relief expected after four days - The News Mill
full_text:
Bihar is experiencing a severe heatwave, with temperatures reaching 40 degrees Celsius and higher, disrupting normal life across the state. The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has warned that these conditions are likely to continue for the next several days.
In Patna, intense sunlight and sustained heat since morning have reduced movement on the roads and affected daily activities. Markets saw fewer visitors, while people sought shade and hydration amid the soaring temperatures. Rickshaw pullers, labourers, and roadside vendors have been among those most severely impacted by the extreme weather.
Electricity consumption has surged due to widespread use of air conditioners and coolers, putting significant strain on power supplies in various areas. Residents have also reported high humidity levels throughout both day and night.
IMD scientist Anand Shankar stated, “Several districts in Bihar are currently experiencing intense heatwave conditions, with temperatures touching 40 degrees Celsius and above. Regarding Bihar, we can say that heatwave conditions prevail, with temperatures around 40 degrees Celsius, and the discomfort level is quite high. Mainly the districts of south-west Bihar like Kaimur, Aurangabad, Rohtas, Buxar, Bhojpur, Arwal, Jehanabad, Gaya, and Nawada are experiencing severe heatwave conditions.”
He added that three zones — North-West, South-West, and South-Central Bihar, including Patna and Nalanda — are witnessing severe heat along with high discomfort caused by humidity. Temperatures in some regions have reached up to 43 degrees Celsius, while moisture from easterly winds has increased the heat index, exacerbating the conditions.
Shankar advised, “People should avoid direct sunlight, especially between 12 PM and 3 PM, and consume adequate fluids to prevent heat-related illnesses. Vulnerable people mainly include the elderly and children, so they need to take more care.”
The IMD expects some relief after four days, with temperatures predicted to decline by 1 to 3 degrees Celsius around May 26 and 27 due to possible thunderstorm activity in parts of the state. While western Bihar remains under heatwave-like conditions, several areas of North-East, North-Central, and South-East Bihar are experiencing regular thunderstorms, which have brought comparatively cooler weather.
During the monsoon season, Shankar noted that Bihar generally receives the monsoon around June 15, with a variation of three to four days, while the expected monsoon onset over Kerala is around May 28.
